title: Early Marriage, Age of Menarche, and Female Schooling Attainment in Bangladesh
abstract: (Article begins on next page) The Harvard community has made this article openly available. Please share how this access benefits you. Your story matters.
summary_en: In much of the world girls are still married very young. This paper uses an unusual natural experiment from Bangladesh — variation in the age of menarche — to estimate the causal effect of delaying marriage on girls' educational attainment. Girls who reached menarche one year later married about 6 months later, and each additional year of postponed marriage raised schooling by 0.22 years and literacy rates by 5.6 percentage points. The result implies that ending child marriage could substantially raise female education — a strong, country-specific causal estimate on an important global policy question.
data_used: Household survey of 1,400 married women in 125 villages in rural Bangladesh collected by the authors; retrospective data on age of menarche, age at marriage, and schooling.
policy_relevance: Primary relevance is to policy in lower-income countries with prevalent child marriage, not directly to Hungary. For Hungarian development-assistance policy (Hungary Helps, IVF cooperation programmes), provides rigorous evidence that legal and normative interventions to delay marriage can pay off in girls' education. Setting-specific: the age-of-menarche instrument is not a policy lever itself; the causal estimate informs what the returns to delaying marriage would be if other levers (education, legal age) succeed.
